notes: Evals for the chunking-strategy-design skill. Prompts are realistic user asks about HOW to chunk documents for a retrieval/RAG system (this skill's scope: boundary placement, size/overlap, structure- and script-aware splitting, chunk metadata, chunk-quality validation, chunking-failure diagnosis), not how to pick the embedding model/reranker, design fusion, or run the eval program (other skills). Evals 1-6 mirror the brief's six required test scenarios (section 11), rewritten as natural user prompts: (1) legal corpus -> structural-first/PGC, (2) product catalog -> attribute-aware, anti domain-generic, (3) CJK -> script-aware not token-count, (4) diagnose bad retrieval -> check chunks before swapping models, (5) quantization -> validate chunking first, (6) multi-hop -> parent-child metadata, defer pipeline. Evals 7-8 guard two other distinctive behaviors: (7) overlap is insurance not a cure -- inspect boundaries before bumping overlap, and (8) semantic-as-default rejection -- structural-first on a structured corpus. Each assertion is objectively checkable by a grader reading the design artifact. Run protocol: each prompt once WITH the skill, once WITHOUT (baseline) -- see RUN_PROTOCOL.md in the workspace.

evals.name: legal-corpus-structural-first
evals.prompt: We've got about 50,000 legal briefs we want to put behind RAG retrieval. They're well structured — each one goes Case Name, then Section, Subsection, down to numbered paragraphs. How should we chunk these?
evals.expected_output: Structural-first strategy: parse the heading hierarchy and subdivide by paragraph within sections (PGC for legal/structured content). Target ~512-768 tokens per paragraph group within the embedding model's optimal range. Heading-path metadata (Case -> Section -> Subsection) on every chunk. No semantic chunking unless evaluation proves structural insufficient. Fixed-character chunking explicitly rejected. Avoids defaulting to '512 tokens with 10% overlap' without structural parsing.
expectations: Recommends structural-first chunking that parses the heading/section hierarchy rather than a fixed-size split
Identifies this as PGC (paragraph-group) territory because legal/structured arguments are organized in paragraph units
Puts heading-path metadata (Case/Section/Subsection) on every chunk
Explicitly rejects fixed-character chunking and does not default to semantic chunking on this structured corpus
Sets a target chunk size within the embedding model's optimal range (e.g. ~512-768 tokens) rather than an unjustified default
Warns against defaulting to a generic '512 tokens with 10% overlap' that ignores the available structure

evals.name: product-catalog-attribute-aware
evals.prompt: Setting up search over our product catalog. Each listing has a SKU, a product name, a description paragraph, and a bunch of spec fields (dimensions, weight, material, etc). What's a good chunking approach here?
evals.expected_output: Attribute-aware structural chunking, NOT paragraph-group or a default 512-token blob. Concatenate SKU + name + description as the chunk text; preserve specification fields as structured metadata for filtering rather than prose to embed. Fixed-character chunking rejected. Notes explicitly that this is a different strategy than narrative or legal documents — domain determines the strategy.
expectations: Recommends attribute-aware / field-aware structural chunking for the structured product listings
Concatenates the identifying text fields (SKU + name + description) as the chunk text
Preserves specification fields as structured metadata for filtering rather than embedding them as prose
Explicitly notes this strategy differs from what a legal/narrative corpus would use (domain determines strategy, no single global default)
Rejects fixed-character chunking and a generic default-token-size approach

evals.name: cjk-script-aware
evals.prompt: We're indexing a few thousand Chinese-language research papers — they've got mixed CJK and English technical terms. How should we configure chunking for these?
evals.expected_output: Script-aware splitter, not token-count-based sizing (which is language-biased and mis-sizes CJK). Character-level sizing for CJK segments. NFC normalization recommended for token efficiency. Use structural boundaries (headings, paragraphs) for semantic coherence where present. Token-count-based chunk sizing explicitly rejected as language-biased. Ideally flags that CJK guidance is lower-confidence / should be validated on a CJK query set.
expectations: Recommends a script-aware splitter rather than token-count-based chunk sizing
Explains that token-count sizing is language-biased and systematically mis-sizes CJK content
Recommends character-level sizing for the CJK segments (and/or NFC normalization)
Still uses structural boundaries (headings, paragraphs) for coherence where the papers provide them
Explicitly rejects applying Latin-script-optimized token-count sizing to the CJK text

evals.name: diagnose-before-swapping-model
evals.prompt: Our RAG retrieval quality is pretty bad. We're currently chunking everything into fixed 256-token pieces with no overlap. We're thinking the fix is to switch to a better embedding model — does that make sense?
evals.expected_output: No — check the chunks first. 256-token fixed-size is the signature of the worst-performing strategy; inspect boundaries for mid-sentence splits. If found, chunking is the root cause, not the embedding model. Recommend a structural-first redesign and measure nDCG on current chunking vs a structural baseline on the SAME model and reranker. Compute chunk-boundary failure rate. Do NOT recommend a model swap until chunking is cleared (misdiagnosing chunking failure as embedding failure).
expectations: Advises checking/diagnosing chunk boundaries BEFORE switching embedding models
Identifies fixed-size (256-token, no overlap) chunking as the likely root cause and inspects for mid-sentence boundaries
Explains that no embedding model can recover information severed at a bad chunk boundary (chunking is the binding constraint)
Recommends a structural-first redesign and comparing it against the current chunking on the same model/reranker (vary only chunking)
Explicitly warns against swapping the embedding model before chunking is ruled out (misdiagnosis of chunking as embedding failure)

evals.name: quantization-validate-chunking-first
evals.prompt: We want to roll out int8 quantization to cut our vector storage costs. Our chunking is 512-token structural right now. Benchmarks say int8 keeps like 94-98% recall, so we should be fine to quantize — agree?
evals.expected_output: Verify chunking quality first. Measure quantization tolerance on PRODUCTION chunking quality, not academic/MTEB benchmarks — the 94-98% figure is from clean chunks and overstates production tolerance because chunking and quantization losses compound multiplicatively. If chunk-boundary failure rate > 5%, fix chunking before quantizing. If chunk quality is good (low boundary-failure rate, chunks stand alone), int8 is reasonable. Warns about the compound-loss interaction.
expectations: Says to verify/validate chunking quality before quantizing
Warns the 94-98% benchmark figure is measured on clean academic chunks and overstates production tolerance
Explains that chunking and quantization losses compound multiplicatively (poor chunking amplifies quantization loss)
Recommends measuring quantization tolerance on the actual production chunking quality, not benchmarks
Conditions the go/no-go on chunk quality (e.g. fix chunking first if boundary-failure rate is high; int8 acceptable only if chunk quality is good)

evals.name: multi-hop-metadata-defer-pipeline
evals.prompt: We're building a RAG system that needs to answer questions pulling information from several documents at once. How should we chunk the documents to support that kind of multi-hop retrieval?
evals.expected_output: Chunking enables multi-hop through metadata, it doesn't design the multi-hop loop. Add parent-child links and adjacent-chunk retrieval support to the metadata schema so answers spanning chunks can be reassembled (prevents multi-hop fragmentation). Configure chunk-to-answer traceability to track multi-hop success. State clearly that the multi-hop retrieval methodology (retrieve -> read -> retrieve more) is owned by Retrieval Pipeline Design — flag the assumption and that it must be verified. Does not design the full multi-hop pipeline inside the chunking artifact.
expectations: Adds parent-child links and/or adjacent-chunk retrieval support to the chunk metadata schema to reassemble cross-document answers
Frames the goal as preventing multi-hop fragmentation (answer spans chunks that aren't linked)
Configures chunk-to-answer traceability / metadata to support tracking multi-hop success
Defers the multi-hop retrieval pipeline (retrieve -> read -> retrieve more) to [[Retrieval Pipeline Design]] rather than designing it here
Flags the cross-reference as an assumption to verify (respects the ownership boundary)

evals.name: overlap-is-not-a-cure
evals.prompt: Our retrieval keeps missing relevant passages and we think chunks are getting cut off. We're planning to bump our chunk overlap from 10% up to 40% to make sure nothing falls through the cracks. Good plan?
evals.expected_output: Pushes back: overlap is insurance, not a cure. If chunks end mid-sentence, 40% overlap just puts the fragment in two chunks — both still fragments. Inspect boundary quality first; if boundaries are bad, fix the strategy (structural-first), don't paper over it with overlap. Flags >20% overlap as a signal that overlap is masking boundary problems. Overlap helps only when boundaries are already good and you want redundant coverage; treat it as a tuning parameter, not a fix.
expectations: Pushes back on raising overlap to 40% as the fix, rather than endorsing it
Explains that overlap masks boundary problems but cannot fix them (a fragment overlapped is still a fragment)
Recommends inspecting/diagnosing chunk boundary quality first before changing overlap
Flags >20% overlap as a red flag that overlap is compensating for bad boundaries
Frames overlap as a tuning parameter for redundant coverage (useful only when boundaries are already good), not a cure for bad chunking

evals.name: semantic-not-the-default
evals.prompt: We're chunking our internal engineering docs for search. They're written in Markdown with clear headings and sections. Our plan is to use embedding-based semantic chunking everywhere since it's the most sophisticated option. Sound right?
evals.expected_output: Recommends structural-first instead: the docs already have clean Markdown headings and sections — author-supplied semantic segmentation — so parse and use that before reaching for semantic chunking. Semantic chunking is a fallback for when structural markers are absent, not the default; it adds embedding cost/complexity and usually doesn't beat structural-first on structured content. Reserve semantic chunking for the unstructured portions if any. Does not endorse semantic-everywhere on a structured corpus.
expectations: Recommends structural-first chunking using the existing Markdown headings/sections rather than semantic chunking everywhere
Explains that headings/sections are author-supplied semantic boundaries that should be exhausted before inventing new ones
States that semantic chunking is a fallback (for when structural markers are absent), not the default starting point
Notes semantic chunking adds cost/complexity and typically does not beat structural-first on already-structured content
Does NOT endorse defaulting to semantic chunking across the whole structured corpus
